comments.body: Personally I wouldn't use Map<String, Object> to represent the patch. I'd just use an instance of Product with null fields. This way it's typesafe and you benefit from code completion etc.

In current implementation I have a request DTO which is used in both creation and updating. It has custom validation annotations for checking if a value is null (and more). I cannot use this dto if I want to send null values so I have to duplicate a DTO in this case for allowing patch fields to be nullable, but I have to duplicate for all my update DTOs in this case. Moreover, implementing different DTO's with validations seems to be overkill because my entity contains 40+ fields. I cannot create a seprate DTO for each update case. What would you suggest in this case?

Perhaps Map<String, Object> is ok. Another option is to generate some logic using an annotation processor. Or perhaps you might want to generate some java code at build time based on your existing model (eg invoking qdox and freemarker via maven)
What library does the @Merger annotation belong to?
This annotation would need to be created by the OP
I cannot understand what this code does. Could you explain it more?

With an annotation processor you can generate whatever code you like. In my example the ProductDelta would contain the changes to a product (eg instead of Map<String, Object>). And the ProductMerger is able to join/merge a Product with a ProductDelta to obtain a new Product instance with both the old and new values. But you could generate whatever code you require via an annotation processor

answers.body:
Personally I prefer type safety so don't like dealing with Map<String, Object>.

You mentioned in a comment that you want to do this for many different DTO's, each with many fields. I think you should consider generating code using an annotation processor. This would allow you to generate an XDelta and an XMerger for each of your model classes.

eg You could annotate the Product class with a custom @Delta annotation

@Target(ElementType.TYPE)
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.SOURCE)
public @interface Delta {
}

@Delta
public class Product {
   @Id
   private Long id;
   
   @NotEmpty 
   private String name;
   
   @GreaterThan(0) 
   private BigDecimal price;
}

The annotation processor would then generate

@Generated("generated by DeltaAnnotationProcessor")
public class ProductDelta {
   public Long getId() { ... }

   public void setName(String name) { ... }
   public boolean isNameSet() { ... }
   public String getName() { ... }

   public void setPrice(BigDecimal price) { ... }
   public boolean isPriceSet() { ... }
   public BigDecimal getPrice() { ... }
}

@Generated("generated by DeltaAnnotationProcessor")
public class ProductMerger {
   public Product merge(Product product, ProductDelta delta) { 
      String name = delta.isNameSet() ? delta.getName() : product.getName();
      BigDecimal price = delta.isPriceSet() ? delta.getPrice() : product.getPrice();
      return new Product(product.getId(), name, price, ...);
   }
}

For generating code I usually use freemarker

This worked for me and you can add new fields to your Product class, they'll be taken into account :

@PatchMapping("/patchParent/{id}")
    public Parent patchParent(@PathVariable Long id, @RequestBody Parent parent) throws IllegalArgumentException, IllegalAccessException {
        
        Optional<Parent> oldParent=repo.findById(id);
        Field[] parentFields = Parent.class.getDeclaredFields();
        
        if(oldParent.isPresent()) {
            for(Field f : parentFields) {
                if(f.getName() != "id") {//we don't need id since it's provided in URL
                    f.setAccessible(true);
                    Object newValue = f.get(parent);
                    if(newValue != null) {
                        f.set(oldParent.get(), newValue);
                    }
                }
            }
            return repo.save(oldParent.get());
        }
        return null;
    }

If you want to change a single field in db and you are completely fetching the row, you need to update the only field which is getting changed and then save into db, this will optimize your code to some extent. If you can put your code here then it will be better.

First I prefer to not use Map<String, Object> with reflection unless you really need something dynamic.

For this question, it is better to create a Patch DTO with nullable fields.

DTO:

@Data
public class ProductPatchDto {
    @Min(0) @Max(100)
    private Integer score;
    private String name;
    private String description;
}

Controller:

@PatchMapping("/{id}")
public ResponseEntity<ProductDto> patchProduct(
        @PathVariable int id,
        @Valid @RequestBody ProductPatchDto patchDto) {
    return ResponseEntity.ok(service.patchProduct(id, patchDto));
}

Service:

public ProductDto patchProduct(int id, ProductPatchDto dto) {
    var product = repo.findById(id).orElseThrow();

    if (dto.getScore() != null) product.setScore(dto.getScore());
    if (dto.getName() != null) product.setName(dto.getName());
    if (dto.getDescription() != null) product.setDescription(dto.getDescription());

    return mapper.toDto(repo.save(product));
}

This way if any field sent this means we should update this field, otherwise keep the actial value.

title: Partial Update using Spring Boot
body:
I am working on a Java Spring Boot project. I have an entity that has 20 fields. I currently have an put method that takes these 20 fields, all of them are non-nulls and I validate them using validators including my custom validators. But it is costly. Even if I want to change a single field in my db, I completely fetch the row, update all fields using custom mapper and save into db. In this implementation, even if only one field changes, I have to send all fields in request and also I overwrite 19 unchanged field into db which is costly. I am looking for solutions to:

  1. I should be able to send only fields that is supposed to change and I should be able to validate them in DTO level using validator (for example: I should be able to check if a values between 0 and 100. In current implementation I have a custom validator for this)
  2. I should be able to overwrite only changed fields into db. I shouldnt overwrite values that should remain same.
  3. (kind of optional) It would be better if it can be generic. I can easily adapt for my different controllers.

I have been searching Java Reflections and found this implementation:

    @PatchMapping("/{id}")
    public Product updateProductFields(@PathVariable int id,@RequestBody Map<String, Object> fields){
        return service.updateProductByFields(id,fields);
    }

    public Product updateProductByFields(int id, Map<String, Object> fields) {
        Optional<Product> existingProduct = repository.findById(id);

        if (existingProduct.isPresent()) {
            fields.forEach((key, value) -> {
                Field field = ReflectionUtils.findField(Product.class, key);
                field.setAccessible(true);
                ReflectionUtils.setField(field, existingProduct.get(), value);
            });
            return repository.save(existingProduct.get());
        }
        return null;
    }


public interface ProductRepository extends
        JpaRepository<Product, Integer> {


}

But I am not sure if this satisfies my desires. Do you have any suggestions?
